nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to Florida, generally reported from freshwater marshes, ditches, slow streams, and seasonally-flooded wetlands of the peninsula south of the panhandle, including parts of the Florida Keys. Tends to occur in littoral, low-flow zones with abundant cover.

decorPreferences: sand or fine gravel substrate (supports burrowing)
abundant dark hiding places (rocks, slate caves, PVC pipe)
artificial plants (live plants are typically uprooted or eaten)
tight-fitting lid with all cord/hose gaps sealed
moderate filtration with shrimp/crayfish-safe intake guard

diet.notes: Generally reported as an opportunistic omnivore that scavenges almost any organic matter. Has been documented accepting sinking shrimp pellets, algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, peas, spinach), bloodworms, frozen brine shrimp, and small pieces of fish or shrimp. Calcium-rich or mineral-supplemented foods are commonly recommended to support successful molting. Juveniles tend to be more carnivorous; adults shift toward a more herbivorous bias.

goodWith: fast mid-water and surface-dwelling fish (zebra danios, giant danios, rainbowfish)
tiger barbs and similar active barbs
large robust tetras kept above the substrate
avoidWith: slow or long-finned fish (bettas, fancy goldfish, gouramis)
live aquarium plants (typically uprooted or eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: Nathan Daly, 3456x2592. Sharp front-quarter aquarium shot of the selectively bred cobalt-blue morph: brilliant electric-blue carapace and claws with white speckling, whole animal in frame and well-lit against rock/gravel. Definitive diagnostic look for this entry.

Nathan Daly, 3456x2592. Same electric-blue morph in an aquarium, head-on emerging from a rock cave. Correct species and clearly blue but darker and partly obscured by decor, so kept as a lower-ranked second angle rather than the pick.

summary: The Electric Blue Crayfish is a selectively bred cobalt-blue form of Procambarus alleni, a Florida native, generally regarded as hardy and easy to feed but aggressive, plant-destructive, and a notorious escape artist requiring a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes: The Electric Blue Crayfish sold in the hobby is a selectively bred color morph of the Florida-endemic Procambarus alleni. Wild specimens range from brown-tan to muted blue, while captive lines have been fixed on the brilliant cobalt coloration seen in shops. The species is generally considered hardy and tolerant of a fairly wide range of water chemistry, though stable parameters are widely regarded as important for successful molting. Like all crayfish, it is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and snail treatments, so any product containing copper should be kept out of a tank housing this species. A tightly sealed lid with no cord or hose gaps is essential — the Electric Blue Crayfish has been widely reported to climb out of aquariums via filter tubing and heater cords, and it dehydrates rapidly once out of water. It tends to rearrange or destroy aquascapes, uprooting and eating most live plants, moving substrate, and toppling loose decor; floating plants are generally the only practical live vegetation. Solitary housing is strongly preferred. Keeping multiple individuals in the same tank typically requires a very large footprint with abundant sight breaks, and occasional fatalities should be expected.
breedingNotes: The Electric Blue Crayfish is generally reported as straightforward to breed in captivity once a compatible pair is established. Sexual maturity is commonly reached around 4–5 cm in body length. Females carry eggs glued to the swimmerets (small paddle-like appendages) under the abdomen — a stage referred to as 'berried' in the hobby — for roughly 3–4 weeks, with reported clutch sizes ranging from around 50 to several hundred eggs depending on the size of the female. Newly hatched juveniles initially cling to the mother before dispersing. Juveniles should be separated from the mother, and ideally from each other, once she stops sheltering them, as cannibalism among siblings and from the female herself is commonly reported once parental care ends.

primary.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (Aquariumbreeder)
primary.notes: Substituted as primary because the assigned Aquarium Co-Op URL (https://www.aquariumcoop.com/blogs/aquarium/electric-blue-crayfish) returned no usable husbandry content (storefront/navigation only). Source of size 10-12 cm, temperature 22-27°C, pH 7.0-8.0 optimal (6.0-9.0 tolerated), gH 6-8 optimal, kH 4-6 optimal, ~5-year lifespan, omnivore diet, aggressive temperament, high escape risk, and molting-frequency ranges (babies few days, juveniles 1-3 weeks, adults 4-8 weeks).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated secondary has no dedicated species page for Procambarus alleni; only tangential coverage in 'illegal crayfish in the UK' news pieces. Substituted with Fish Laboratory and Wikipedia for cross-verification.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Secondary substitute. Cross-confirmed adult size 4-6 in (10-15 cm), lifespan 2-6 years, temperature 20-26°C, pH 6.5-7.5, aggressive temperament, high escape risk, 30 US gal (~110 L) tank minimum, and plant-destructive behavior. Tank size differed from primary (20 vs 30 gal); larger value used per project convention.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Procambarus alleni)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback for family Cambaridae / order Decapoda, native range (Florida east of St Johns River and southwards including parts of the Florida Keys), and confirmation that the 'Electric Blue' aquarium form is a selectively-bred captive color line of an otherwise brown-to-muted-blue wild species.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts roughly every 4-8 weeks as an adult; juveniles molt much more frequently (every 1-3 weeks) and very young hatchlings every few days. Complete hardening of the new exoskeleton has been reported to take about 3 days, during which the crayfish is highly vulnerable and tends to hide. Discarded exoskeletons are commonly consumed for calcium and should generally be left in the tank. Insufficient mineral content (low gH/kH or low dietary calcium) and unstable water parameters are widely associated with incomplete or fatal molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn readily in stable conditions. See top-level breedingNotes for clutch size, incubation, and sibling-cannibalism details.
